expectations.text: Reads and references the scope doc — skips problem/motivation domain since it was resolved in scope
expectations.evidence: The transcript context mentions the scope was 'already approved' and the PRD's Non-Goals section mirrors scope exclusions accurately, but there is no evidence the assistant read the file plans/digital-waivers-scope.md (the eval prompt explicitly directed it there). Furthermore, the PRD does NOT skip the problem/motivation domain -- it includes a full 'Problem Statement' section and a 'Goals' section re-deriving motivation from scratch rather than referencing the scope doc. The expectation requires both reading the scope doc and skipping problem/motivation since it was already resolved, and neither is clearly evidenced.

expectations.text: No technical implementation details (schemas, APIs, architecture, build phases, technology selection)
expectations.evidence: The PRD is heavily technical. It contains: (1) a full Data Model section with four database table definitions including column names, types, and foreign keys (lines 170-252); (2) an API/Actions table listing 13 endpoints with inputs and permission mappings (lines 257-274); (3) technology selection references: 'Effect-based action pattern,' 'S3-compatible object store (R2 recommended given Cloudflare tunnel setup),' 'Next.js app router,' 'Drizzle for schema,' 'Bun runtime' (quality gate lines 26-27); (4) a three-phase Rollout Plan with weekly build phases (lines 312-332); (5) ID prefix conventions (lines 239-245); (6) URL routing patterns like '/settings/waivers/[templateId]' (lines 280-294). This reads more like a technical design document than a product requirements document.

expectations.text: Covers all 9 PRD domains (problem skip OK if scope exists): target users, user stories, functional requirements, non-functional requirements, success metrics, scope, dependencies, risks
expectations.evidence: Domains present: target users (Personas section, line 39), functional requirements (FR-1 through FR-5, lines 50-165), non-functional requirements (table at lines 300-308), scope (Non-Goals section, lines 26-33), risks (Open Questions section, lines 335-341). Domains MISSING: (1) User stories -- completely absent, the PRD jumps from Personas to Functional Requirements with no user stories at all; (2) Success metrics -- no metrics table or section defining how success will be measured (no KPIs, no targets, no measurement plan); (3) Dependencies & Constraints -- no dedicated section, only oblique references in Open Questions (SMS infrastructure, object storage). That is 3 of 9 domains missing.

expectations.text: User stories follow As-a/I-want/So-that format with acceptance criteria (checkboxes)
expectations.evidence: The PRD contains zero user stories. There is no section titled 'User Stories' and no text anywhere in the document using As-a/I-want/So-that format. The PRD has a Personas section (lines 39-47) and jumps directly to Functional Requirements (line 50). No acceptance criteria with checkboxes exist anywhere in the document.

expectations.text: Quality gate automatically runs after PRD — delivers Ready/Revise/Rethink verdict with strengths and issues
expectations.evidence: quality-gate.md exists in the outputs directory. It contains a structured review with 9 criteria sections, each with a Pass/concern assessment. It identifies strengths (e.g., 'Problem statement is concrete and grounded in real operational pain,' 'Four new tables are well-defined') and issues (SMS delivery unresolved, no object storage in codebase, no PDF library chosen). The verdict is: 'Ready for technical design with minor follow-ups.' However, the verdict uses a custom phrasing rather than the expected Ready/Revise/Rethink trichotomy -- it says 'Ready for technical design with minor follow-ups' which is closest to 'Ready' but not the exact framework. Passing because a verdict with strengths and issues is clearly delivered.

expectations.text: Glossary conditions checked after quality gate — generates glossary if 3+ domain nouns or naming conflicts detected
expectations.evidence: No evidence of glossary condition checking anywhere in the transcript or output files. The quality-gate.md does not mention glossary, domain nouns, or naming conflicts. No glossary file was generated. The PRD itself introduces several domain-specific terms (waiver template, signed waiver, guardian flow, material change, superseded, voided) that would likely trigger a glossary check, but no such check occurred.

expectations.text: Suggests /design (UI features) or /architect (backend-only) as next step
expectations.evidence: Neither the PRD, quality-gate.md, nor the transcript contain any mention of /design, /architect, or a suggested next skill/step. The quality gate concludes with 'Resolve the three open questions... within the first week as prerequisite decisions for Phase 2' but does not suggest a pipeline next step.

expectations.text: PRD has key sections: Problem Statement, Target Users (with personas), User Stories, Functional Requirements (FR-N numbered), Non-Functional Requirements, Success Metrics table, Scope (in/out/future), Dependencies & Constraints, Risks & Open Questions, Pipeline Status
expectations.evidence: Sections present: Problem Statement (line 16), Target Users with personas (lines 39-47, three personas defined), Functional Requirements with FR-N numbering (FR-1 through FR-5, lines 50-165), Non-Functional Requirements (table at lines 299-308), Risks & Open Questions (lines 335-341). Sections MISSING: (1) User Stories -- absent entirely; (2) Success Metrics table -- no metrics, KPIs, or measurement targets anywhere; (3) Scope as in/out/future format -- Non-Goals lists out-of-scope items but there is no 'in scope' enumeration or 'future' category; (4) Dependencies & Constraints -- no dedicated section; (5) Pipeline Status -- no pipeline status section. 5 of 10 required sections are missing or do not match the expected format.

claims.claim: The PRD covers all six scope areas: template management, mobile signing, e-signature, status tracking, expiration, minor/guardian signing
claims.evidence: Quality gate review criterion 3 confirms all six scope areas are addressed in functional requirements FR-1 through FR-5.

claims.claim: Existing permission model already defines waiver_template and waiver resources
claims.evidence: metrics.json lists 'permissions.ts (existing waiver_template and waiver permissions)' as a codebase artifact referenced. The PRD maps actions to these existing permission resources.

claims.claim: Stack is compatible: Next.js app router, Drizzle, Effect, better-auth
claims.evidence: metrics.json shows codebase artifacts referenced including 'services.ts (Effect service pattern)', 'auth.ts schema', and the quality gate confirms compatibility at line 26.

claims.claim: PRD is ready for technical design
claims.evidence: The quality gate verdict says 'Ready for technical design with minor follow-ups.' The PRD is indeed detailed enough -- arguably too detailed, including a full database schema and API surface that belong in technical design rather than a PRD.

claims.claim: No cron dependency for MVP (expiration evaluated at read time)
claims.evidence: FR-4.2 (line 141) states: 'Expiration is evaluated at read time (no background job needed for MVP). A waiver is expired if signedAt + expirationDays < now.'

suggestions.assertion: Reads and references the scope doc — skips problem/motivation domain since it was resolved in scope
suggestions.reason: This assertion conflates two things: (1) reading the scope doc, and (2) skipping problem/motivation. These should be separate assertions. A baseline run without the skill likely has no scope doc to read, making the 'reads scope doc' part dependent on the test harness setup. Consider splitting into 'References scope doc decisions in PRD' and 'Does not re-derive problem/motivation already settled in scope.'

suggestions.assertion: Quality gate automatically runs after PRD — delivers Ready/Revise/Rethink verdict with strengths and issues
suggestions.reason: The quality gate here used a custom verdict ('Ready for technical design with minor follow-ups') rather than the Ready/Revise/Rethink trichotomy. The assertion passed because the spirit was met, but a stricter eval might check for the exact verdict vocabulary. Also, 'automatically runs' cannot be verified from outputs alone -- we can only confirm the output exists, not whether it was triggered automatically or manually.

No assertion checks the PRD's length or verbosity. This output is ~15K chars with full database schemas and API tables -- it reads more like a technical design doc than a product requirements doc. An assertion measuring whether the output stays at product altitude (complementing the 'no technical details' absence check) would catch this important quality dimension.

No assertion checks interview quality -- whether the right questions were asked, whether the interview was efficient, or whether it used structured tools (AskUserQuestion). The transcript shows 12 questions asked in a conversational style. For a 'with_skill' vs 'without_skill' comparison, interview conduct is a key differentiator worth measuring.

eval_feedback.overall: The assertions are well-designed to test the skill's PRD pipeline (scope reference, product altitude, domain coverage, quality gate, glossary, next step). For a 'without_skill' baseline, the low pass rate (1/8) is expected -- an unguided assistant naturally drifts into technical details and misses the structured PRD template. The main gap is that several assertions test skill-specific pipeline features (glossary check, /design suggestion, pipeline status) that a vanilla assistant would never produce, making them automatic fails rather than discriminating tests. Consider separating 'pipeline behavior' assertions from 'output quality' assertions so the baseline score better reflects what a skilled assistant adds beyond pipeline mechanics.
